Drag budget — who eats the fuel

what am I looking at?

The particles are air. Their motion is not an artist's guess: every vector comes from this project's 2-D source-panel solution — the same mathematics that produces the drag numbers in the repository. Bright particles are fast air; by Bernoulli, fast air is low pressure.

The nose: air piles up and stops — the stagnation point, the highest pressure on the car, pushing it backwards.

The roof: air accelerates (watch it brighten). Low pressure here mostly pulls up, not back.

The hatched region is the wake. Past the separation point (orange marker) the flow lets go of the body and the mathematics of smooth flow stops being true — so this page honestly stops drawing it. The car drags that pocket of slow, low-pressure air along with it, and paying for that pocket is roughly 60% of your aerodynamic fuel bill. Every modification this project recommends is an attempt to shrink it or raise its pressure.

Try it: press 1, then 2. The sedan's tapered tail hands the air back gently — small base, small hatched wake, Cd 0.266. The hatch ends in a wall — tall base, tall wake, Cd 0.344. Wake size sets drag, and that is the lever every modification in this project pulls.

Then press 2, 3, 4 and notice what does NOT happen: the numbers barely move. In silhouette a hatch and an SUV are almost the same shape, so a 2-D model prices them almost the same — while the real Nexon and Scorpio are measurably draggier for reasons a side view cannot see: squared body corners, roof rails, flared arches, mirrors. That gap is the documented boundary of this method (quantified in the repository's validation table), shown here rather than hidden. A tool that displays its own limits is one you can trust about everything else it shows.

What this page will not show you: an earlier version had an "add a diffuser" toggle. The raw flow field priced the diffuser at slightly worse drag — correctly, in its own terms, because a diffuser's benefit is pressure recovery inside the wake, which is viscous physics that smooth-flow mathematics cannot contain. The repository models that effect separately, on a measured budget with error bars. The toggle was cut rather than faked; a picture that lies pleasantly is worse than no picture.

Honesty line: this is potential-flow physics with a base-pressure wake model, not CFD. Streamlines are drawn only where that mathematics is valid; the wake is hatched because there the solver switches to a modelled constant pressure (Cpb shown in the readouts). The model's documented boundary — what it can and cannot predict — is in the repository README, next to the tests that enforce it.
